title: Potato scones
subtitle: Scottish tattie scones — griddle-cooked flat potato bread
excerpt: Scottish tattie scones made from cold mashed potato, a little flour, and butter, cooked on a hot dry griddle in minutes. They are not scones in the cream-tea sense: flat, soft, and slightly chewy with a floury crust. Eaten warm with butter at breakfast or served with a full Scottish.
sourceNotes: Potato scones (tattie scones) are a Scottish staple documented in Scottish domestic cookbooks from the eighteenth century onwards. Elizabeth Cleland, A New and Easy Method of Cookery (1755), includes potato-based griddle breads among the Scottish flat breads. The basic formula of cold mashed potato, a small amount of flour, and fat cooked on a hot griddle has remained unchanged. The recipe here follows the established Scottish domestic formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze once cool between sheets of baking paper. Reheat in a dry griddle pan or toaster.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles straightforwardly. Cook in batches on the griddle.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make and cook the day before. Reheat in a dry pan or toaster. They keep for two days refrigerated.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: cooked on a hot dry griddle or cast-iron pan over medium-high heat, not in an oven. The griddle is ready when a pinch of flour flicked onto it turns golden in 10 seconds.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Scottish griddle-cooked flat bread made from cold mashed potato and a small amount of flour. Unlike cream tea scones, tattie scones are flat, soft, and slightly dense — they rise very little because there is no leavening. The potato must be cold and well-mashed with no lumps; warm or wet potato produces a sticky dough that tears when rolled. Use floury potato varieties such as Maris Piper or King Edward.

content.text: is nothing like a cream tea scone. It is flat, soft, and cooked on a hot griddle in under 4 minutes per side. The potato must be cold — hot or warm mashed potato makes the dough sticky and difficult to roll.

attrs.prepNote: floury variety such as Maris Piper, boiled, mashed and cooled completely

content.text: Combine the cold mashed potato, flour, melted butter, and salt in a bowl and mix to a soft, slightly sticky dough. It will be drier than a bread dough but should just hold together when pressed. Divide into four portions. On a well-floured surface, roll each portion to a circle of about 5 mm thickness. Cut each circle into quarters to make four triangles.

Heat a dry griddle pan or heavy frying pan over medium-high heat until very hot — a pinch of flour flicked onto the surface should turn golden in about 10 seconds. Cook the tattie scones in batches, without oil or butter, for 3 to 4 minutes per side until the surface is deep golden-brown in patches and the scone feels dry and firm when pressed. They do not puff. Serve warm with butter, or cool and reheat in a toaster or dry pan.
